statusLine: Americas crude markets show modest stability with WTI up 0.84% week-on-week to $84.88/bbl, though Middle East shipping risks and significant US inventory draws signal underlying supply tensions.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets displayed cautious strength in the week ending June 13, with WTI gaining 0.84% to $84.88/bbl while maintaining its typical discount to Brent at $2.45. This modest weekly advance comes as US commercial crude inventories fell sharply by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels, indicating healthy domestic demand despite production holding steady at 13.8 million bpd—maintaining America's position as the world's largest oil producer.

The inventory draw extended beyond crude oil, with distillate stocks declining 200,000 barrels to 102.1 million barrels, though gasoline inventories edged up slightly by 186,000 barrels to 215.1 million barrels. These mixed signals in refined products reflect typical seasonal patterns as the summer driving season approaches, with retail gasoline prices dropping $0.158 to $4.281/gallon and diesel falling $0.14 to $5.21/gallon—providing some relief to consumers and logistics operators.

Critical maritime security risks continue to threaten global energy flows, with multiple MARAD advisories highlighting ongoing Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait, plus Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz. While these chokepoints primarily affect Middle East-to-Europe flows rather than Americas supply routes, any disruption to global crude flows could impact WTI pricing through arbitrage opportunities and shifting trade patterns.

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at 349.2 million barrels, representing about 40% of its historical capacity, as the Biden administration continues gradual rebuilding efforts. With geopolitical tensions persisting and inventory draws accelerating, Americas energy markets face a delicate balance between adequate supply buffers and growing demand as economic activity rebounds across the Western Hemisphere.
keyPoints: WTI crude gained 0.84% week-on-week to $84.88/bbl in the week ending June 13, with the WTI-Brent spread at $2.45
US commercial crude inventories fell sharply by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels, signaling strong domestic demand
US oil production held steady at 13.8 million bpd, maintaining America's position as the world's largest producer
Retail fuel prices declined with gasoline down $0.158 to $4.281/gallon and diesel falling $0.14 to $5.21/gallon
Critical maritime advisories remain active for Red Sea and Persian Gulf shipping routes, posing ongoing supply chain risks
Strategic Petroleum Reserve stands at 349.2 million barrels as gradual rebuilding continues
